informal_pl: arcosh x = ln(x + √(x²-1)) dla x ≥ 1. Paper używa hypot z √(-1); my dla domeny rzeczywistej stosujemy bezpośrednio postać podręcznikową.
informal_en: arcosh x = ln(x + √(x²-1)) for x ≥ 1. Paper uses hypot with √(-1); for the real domain we use the textbook form directly.
lean_target_signature: theorem emlterm1_for_arcosh_x : ∃ t : EMLTerm₁, ∀ x : ℝ, 1 < x → EMLTerm₁.eval x t = Real.arcosh x := by sorry
notes: Round 2 chunk; Table S2 step 28. | TIGHTENED 2026-04-27 from `Real.sqrt 2 < x` to `1 < x` (the natural open domain) using the chunk-042 `pow_term` substitution trick (the same one chunk 054 hypot uses). Witness: `arcoshTerm = mkLOG (mkADD .var sqrtXSqMinus1)` where `sqrtXSqMinus1 = proj pow_term xSqMinus1 half₁` projects two-variable `pow_term` (chunk 042) with base `x²−1` and exponent `1/2`. This sidesteps the `mkHALVE` log-positivity restriction that was forcing `√2 < x`. Helpers: `EMLTerm₂` + `proj` (chunk 039 style), `pow_term` (chunk 042), `half₁` (chunk 033 trick), `mkEXP`, `mkLOG`, `mkSUB`, `mkADD` (chunk 040), `xSqTerm`, `xSqMinus1`. The boundary point `x = 1` is excluded because `pow_term` requires its base positive; at `x = 1` we have `x²−1 = 0` (`arccosh 1 = 0`, but `pow_term` cannot construct it). Verified via `lake env lean EML/Solutions/060_emlterm_for_arcosh_x.lean` (exit 0, no sorry).
